narrative:
As a white male applicant with a solid but not standout academic background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, family income, and study plans to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 22, lower-middle family income bracket, Year 1 school decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 3, and part-time enrollment status—and calculated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence to maintain their bar passage rates.

The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score of 22, which is substantially below the average of 36.7 seen in the dataset; this stood out as the top driver, signaling to the model a potential gap in the standardized reasoning skills crucial for bar success. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your family income bracket in the lower-middle range, which had a favorable influence by nudging the prediction toward passing, as students from similar backgrounds in the data often showed resilience and motivation that aided their performance. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, lower than the typical 3.21 average, also pulled positively toward a pass prediction but with less force than your income, helping to mitigate some of the LSAT's impact without fully offsetting it.

Compared to typical students in the dataset, who often had stronger test scores and full-time enrollment (92.7% were full-time versus your part-time status), your profile aligned more closely with those at higher risk of first-time bar failure, even though your later-year deciles suggested some academic recovery. This combination explained why the model leaned toward rejection despite the near-50% odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, as that's the single biggest lever here; supplementing with targeted prep courses or full-time study commitments could also strengthen future applications. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and reapply once you've built a more competitive edge. Your determination as a part-time aspiring lawyer positions you well to turn this around.
